content.problemStatement:
You have a chocolate bar divided into NNN chunks, where each chunk has a sweetness value. You need to share this chocolate with KKK friends by making KKK cuts, resulting in K+1K+1K+1 pieces. Each piece must contain consecutive chunks. You'll eat the piece with the minimum total sweetness and give the rest to your friends. Your task is to find the maximum possible sweetness you can get for your piece.


Input Format

First line contains TTT, the number of test cases
For each test case:

First line contains two integers NNN and KKK (1≤N≤104,1≤K≤N−11 ≤ N ≤ 10^4, 1 ≤ K ≤ N-11≤N≤104,1≤K≤N−1)
Second line contains NNN integers representing the sweetness of each chunk (1≤sweetness[i]≤1051 ≤ sweetness[i] ≤ 10^51≤sweetness[i]≤105)




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer representing the maximum possible minimum sweetness you can get.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and K (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^4, 0 ≤ K ≤ N‑1) – the number of chocolate chunks and the number of cuts you will make. The second line contains N integers a1, a2, …, aN (1 ≤ ai ≤ 10^5) representing the sweetness of each chunk.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the maximum possible sweetness of the piece you will eat (i.e., the maximum achievable value of the minimum total sweetness among the K+1 consecutive pieces).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: optimal division → [1,2,3] (sum 6), [4,5] (9), [6] (6), [7] (7), [8] (8), [9] (9). Minimum piece sum = 6.
Test case 2: with 8 cuts we must have 9 single‑chunk pieces, the smallest chunk is 1.
Test case 3: divide into three equal‑sum parts [1,2,2] (5), [1,2,2] (5), [1,2,2] (5). Minimum = 5.
Any division into 3 pieces leaves at least one piece with sum 1, so the maximum possible minimum sweetness is 1.
We can split as [2,2], [2,2], [2,2]; each piece sums to 4, which is the largest achievable minimum.

solution.approach: The problem asks for the largest value X such that we can split the array into at least K+1 consecutive pieces, each having sum ≥ X. For a fixed X we can check feasibility greedily: walk through the array, keep a running sum, and whenever the sum reaches X we cut a piece and reset the sum. The number of pieces obtained this way is the maximum possible for that X. If we obtain ≥ K+1 pieces, X is feasible; otherwise it is not. Because feasibility is monotone (if X works, any smaller value also works), we binary‑search X between 1 and the total sum of the array. The highest feasible X is the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log S) per test case, where S is the total sweetness (≤ N·10^5). The binary search performs O(log S) iterations and each iteration scans the array once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.
